name: Style vector extraction with author-conditioned QLoRA fine-tuning
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

This conversation is part of an ongoing deep learning course project for Dharmik Mistry (student ID 202311039) at DA-IICT, Gandhinagar, working toward an M.Tech in ICT (ML). The project extends the StyleVector paper with a novel cold-start contribution, and this session focused on consolidating all prior context from an uploaded conversation history zip file, then moving into environment setup and scraper implementation.

The session began with Dharmik asking for a discussion of dataset choices across project stages rather than immediate code. Claude confirmed LaMP-4 as the dataset for QLoRA fine-tuning and style vector extraction, and the custom Indian dataset (TOI + HT journalists) as the cold-start test set, with All The News dropped entirely as unnecessary. Dharmik then shared screenshots of TOI and HT author pages alongside an author registry JSON file, which confirmed the scraping strategy: Playwright for TOI profile pages (which use a "LOAD MORE STORIES" button requiring JavaScript interaction) and requests plus BeautifulSoup for HT profile pages (which use standard numbered pagination). The author registry contains 25 TOI and 30 HT authors, with 6 TOI desk accounts and 3 HT desk accounts to filter, leaving 46 usable named individual journalists. Dharmik's stated goal is to collect as many articles as possible per author, then classify authors into rich versus sparse categories after scraping rather than capping collection upfront.

Claude built the full project structure matching Dharmik's specified directory layout (DL/ with data/, scraping/, model/, src/, configs/, notebooks/, tests/, logs/, outputs/), wrote the TOI scraper using Playwright with resume-safe append logic, the HT scraper using requests with numbered page iteration, shared scraping utilities including validation requiring headline of at least 10 characters, body of at least 100 words, and date present, plus a project log markdown file for tracking all runs and results. When Dharmik ran pip install on Windows with his existing conda environment (Python 3.13), two errors occurred: pandas 2.2.2 cannot build from source on Python 3.13 due to missing stdalign.h in the MSVC compiler, and lxml 5.2.1 conflicts with trafilatura 1.9.0 which requires lxml below 5.2.0. Claude's fix was to create a new conda environment named cold_start_sv with Python 3.10, install pandas and numpy via conda rather than pip to avoid Cython compilation, and drop the pinned lxml version from the pip install command so trafilatura resolves it automatically. Dharmik also encountered a syntax error from copying a multi-line verification command as a single string, which Claude resolved by providing each import check as a separate one-line command.

attachments.extracted_content:
Before I do anything else, let me be straight with you about several things in this plan that need to be challenged, then I'll give you the full compute comparison including Lightning AI.

---

## ⚠️ REVIEW — What's Actually Wrong With This Plan

**Issue 1 — LLaMA-2 is a dead choice in 2026.**
The plan is anchored to LLaMA-2-7B, which requires Meta approval and is two generations behind. LLaMA-3.1-8B or Mistral-7B-v0.3 are ungated, better at instruction following, and have longer context windows. There is no good reason to use LLaMA-2 right now. If your faculty specifically requires it, get that in writing, because it's going to cost you time waiting for access that you don't need to wait for.

**Issue 2 — The training objective directly contradicts the extraction goal.**
The plan has you fine-tune the model to generate *style-agnostic* generic headlines, then immediately tries to extract *style signals* from its activations. Think about what you've done: you've trained the model to suppress style. The hidden states at any layer during generic headline generation are going to reflect "generic mode" — exactly the mode where style information is least present. You're then trying to use those activations as style representations. This is the central design flaw, and nobody flagged it in the original plan.

The correct approach: extract activations on the *author's real article + real headline* pairs using the base model (not fine-tuned for style suppression), or fine-tune the model to be *style-aware* (e.g., with author-conditioned prompts like `"Write in the style of [author_name]:"`) so activations encode style by design.

**Issue 3 — Clustering 30–40 points in 4096 dimensions is not research, it's noise.**
The plan correctly flags this as Risk 1 but then hand-waves the mitigation. You cannot cluster 30–40 vectors in 4096D space and call the result meaningful. You need either (a) dimensionality reduction first — PCA to 50–100 dimensions before clustering, or (b) a much larger rich-author set from All The News (200+ journalists). The entire cold-start contribution rests on this clustering being meaningful. If it isn't, you have no paper.

**Issue 4 — You haven't told me where you currently are.**
Is any of this done? Have you scraped data? Requested model access? Written any code? I need to know your actual starting point before I help you execute anything.

---

## 🧭 APPROACH — Lightning AI vs Kaggle vs Colab

Here's the honest comparison. This is the one thing missing from the original plan.

| Feature | Kaggle Free | Colab Free | **Lightning AI Free** |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPU | P100 16GB | T4 16GB | **T4 16GB or L4 24GB** |
| Weekly quota | 30h/week | ~12h session | **22 credits/month** |
| Session stability | Stable | Disconnects randomly | **Stable** |
| Persistent storage | No (output only) | No | **Yes — Studio persists** |
| Interface | Notebooks only | Notebooks only | **VS Code + Jupyter** |
| Resume from crash | Manual | Manual | **Near-seamless** |
| Secret management | Kaggle Secrets | Drive / env vars | **Studio env vars** |
| Best for | Competition notebooks | Quick experiments | **Multi-day training runs** |

**For your specific project, Lightning AI wins on two critical axes:**

**Persistence:** Your activation extraction in Week 4 will take 8–12 hours minimum. On Colab, one random disconnect wipes your session state. On Kaggle, you can save output files but your environment resets. On Lightning AI, your Studio — packages, files, environment — persists between sessions. You can close the browser and come back tomorrow. This alone makes it the right choice for Week 4.

**L4 GPU:** Lightning's L4 (24GB VRAM) handles QLoRA fine-tuning of LLaMA-3.1-8B with batch size 8 + gradient accumulation 4, no issues. The P100 on Kaggle has older architecture that's slower on bf16 operations, which is the precision you'll use for QLoRA. The L4 is roughly 2x faster on transformer workloads.

**The right platform split:**
- **Scraping, EDA, preprocessing, evaluation code**: local machine or Lightning Studio CPU (free)
- **QLoRA fine-tuning (Week 3)**: Lightning AI L4 — persistent, fast, save directly to Studio
- **Activation extraction (Week 4)**: Lightning AI L4 — crash recovery is the deciding factor
- **Competition notebook baseline** (if you have a parallel Kaggle project): Kaggle
- **Deployment**: HuggingFace Spaces + Vercel as the plan says — this part is correct

**Lightning AI free tier specifics (as of early 2026):**
- 22 GPU credits/month free
- L4 GPU costs ~1.2 credits/hour → ~18 hours L4 per month on free tier
- Studios are persistent (filesystem, installed packages)
- You can buy additional credits cheaply if you exceed the free quota during heavy weeks

**One real limitation:** 22 GPU hours/month is tight if Week 3 training takes 10 hours and Week 4 extraction takes 12 hours. That's 22 hours right there, with nothing left for debugging runs. Mitigation: use Kaggle for the fine-tuning exploratory runs (cheap GPU time, disposable), then do the final proper run on Lightning AI with the tuned config.

---

## ➡️ NEXT STEP — What I Need From You Before Writing Code

Answer these before I write a single line:

1. **Current status:** What exists right now? Working scraper? Any data collected? LLaMA access requested?

2. **Model decision:** Are you locked to LLaMA-2 by a faculty requirement, or can you switch to LLaMA-3.1-8B or Mistral-7B? This changes everything about the setup.

3. **The training objective:** Do you understand the flaw I described in Issue 2 above? What's your response to it? I want to make sure we fix this before you write any training code.

4. **Clustering fix:** Do you have access to All The News V2 data, and are you willing to expand the rich-author pool to 200+ journalists to make the clustering statistically defensible?

5. **Lightning AI account:** Do you have one? If not, create it at `lightning.ai` now — it takes 5 minutes and you'll need it for Week 3.

Give me these answers and I'll immediately start generating the Week 1 scraper code with proper author filtering, date validation, and the corrected activation extraction design.

## ⚠️ REVIEW — You're Asking the Wrong Question

"Which model is better" sounds like one question. It's actually three completely separate decisions, and conflating them will waste weeks of your time. Let me break this down precisely.

The paper uses **two models**, not one:
- **M** — the model being steered (activation intervention happens here)
- **Mg** — the generic model that generates style-agnostic responses (no intervention, just inference)

Your cold-start extension adds a third concern: which model's activation space produces style vectors that cluster well enough to enable interpolation?

These three choices have different answers.

---

## 🧭 APPROACH — The Actual Decision Matrix

### Decision 1: Base Model M (the one being steered)

This is your core choice. The paper locked to LLaMA-2-7B-chat. Here's the honest comparison of your real options in 2026:

| Model | Gating | Context | Style Sep. | HW Fit (16GB) | Comparability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LLaMA-2-7B-chat** | ✗ Meta approval | 4K | Paper baseline | ✅ 8-bit fits | Direct paper comparison |
| **LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct** | ✅ Ungated | 128K | Likely better | ✅ 8-bit fits | New baseline needed |
| **Mistral-7B-v0.3-Instruct** | ✅ Ungated | 32K | Comparable | ✅ 8-bit fits | New baseline needed |
| **Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct** | ✅ Ungated | 128K | Strong | ✅ 8-bit fits | New baseline needed |

**The honest answer: use LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct.**

Here's why, and I'm not hedging:

**First**, your dataset is Indian news articles, not LaMP/LongLaMP. You cannot directly compare your numbers to Table 2 in the paper regardless of which model you pick — your task, domain, and data are all different. The comparability argument for LLaMA-2 is therefore void.

**Second**, LLaMA-3.1-8B has a 128K context window versus LLaMA-2's 4K. Indian news articles can be long. You will hit context limits with LLaMA-2 on full articles during activation extraction. LLaMA-3.1 eliminates this problem entirely.

**Third**, better base models tend to have more linearly separable representations — this is the core geometric property that StyleVector depends on (see Figure 4 in the paper: AUC > 0.85 across all layers). A model with cleaner representation geometry gives you more separable style vectors. Your cold-start interpolation will only work if the cluster structure in activation space is meaningful — a stronger model increases the odds.

**Fourth**, no approval wait. You cannot afford 1–3 days of blocking on LLaMA-2 access. LLaMA-3.1-8B is downloadable right now.

**The one legitimate argument for LLaMA-2:** if your faculty or course rubric specifically says "reproduce StyleVector" and evaluates you on matching the paper's exact numbers. If that's the case, tell me now — but even then, I'd argue that using LLaMA-3.1 and showing the method transfers is a stronger academic contribution, not a weaker one.

---

### Decision 2: Generic Model Mg (style-agnostic generator)

The paper's Appendix C.2 (Figure 7) already answers this for you. Look at what they found:

- More powerful Mg → better style vector extraction
- GPT-3.5-Turbo and DeepSeek-Chat outperform LLaMA-2-7b as Mg
- The generic model does **not** need to match M

**Use Gemini 2.0 Flash as Mg.** It's free on the API tier, stronger than GPT-3.5-Turbo for factual content generation, and handles Indian news articles well. You'll be generating style-agnostic headlines for every article in your corpus — that's 2,000–4,000 API calls. Gemini Flash free tier handles this without cost.

Do NOT use the same LLaMA-3.1-8B as both M and Mg. You want the generic responses to come from a model with a different generation style — this maximizes the contrast in activation space and gives you a cleaner style signal.

---

### Decision 3: Which Model's Activations Support Cold-Start Clustering

This is what nobody in the plan addressed, and it's the crux of your novel contribution.

Cold-start interpolation works only if style vectors from different authors cluster into interpretable groups in the model's activation space. The paper's Figure 8 shows this works for LLaMA-2-7B on LaMP data. You need to verify it works for your model on your data — this is not guaranteed.

The property you need is: **same-author vectors close together, different-author vectors farther apart, and authors with similar styles forming geometrically coherent clusters.**

LLaMA-3.1-8B is a better bet here than LLaMA-2 because its training used more diverse text and its attention layers are more refined — the internal representations are generally cleaner. But you must validate this empirically in Week 5 before building the cold-start interpolation on top of it. If clustering fails, no model choice saves you. Run the silhouette score sweep early.

---

## The Answer, Directly

| Role | Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **M** (base, steered) | LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct | Ungated, 128K ctx, better representations, no approval wait |
| **Mg** (generic generator) | Gemini 2.0 Flash API | Free, strong, different generation style from M |
| **Fallback if LLaMA-3.1 fails clustering** | Mistral-7B-v0.3 | Different architecture, worth trying if activation geometry is poor |

---

## ➡️ NEXT STEP

Now answer the three questions I asked you last time, because I still don't have them:

1. What is your current status — what exists, what's been started?
2. Is LLaMA-2 a hard faculty requirement or just the plan's default assumption?
3. Do you have a Lightning AI account yet?

These answers determine which week we start coding from. Without them I'm writing code for the wrong starting point.

## ⚠️ REVIEW — Parsing What You Actually Said

Answer 3 has a subtle but important implication I need to call out before it causes problems later.

"Option B but finetune the weights too" — you can't do full fine-tuning of 8B parameters on an L4. That's not a hardware complaint, that's physics. 8B parameters in float32 = 32GB just for weights, before gradients or optimizer states. **QLoRA is not a compromise — it IS the weight update.** LoRA adapters modify the effective weight matrices via low-rank decomposition. The model's behavior changes, the course sees backpropagation happening, the weights that matter get updated. This is what Option B with QLoRA means and it's the right call.

Now, your Answer 2 opened something important: **LaMP dataset is actually better than All The News for this project.** Here's why I'm changing my own recommendation from last session.

---

## 🧭 APPROACH — The Revised Architecture

The LaMP benchmark is the exact dataset the paper evaluated on. It has **2,376 users for News Headline Generation alone**, with an average of 287 articles per user (Table 4 in the paper you uploaded). That's not just enough for clustering — it's the ground truth comparison set. Using it means:

- Direct comparability with the paper's Table 2 numbers
- Pre-structured (user, article, headline) triples — no scraping
- Available via HuggingFace `datasets` — one line of code
- Enough rich authors for statistically meaningful clustering

The Indian dataset (TOI + HT) is now the **novel contribution domain** — you're showing the method transfers to a different language register, publication culture, and journalism style. That's a stronger academic story than just replicating on Western data.

Here is the complete revised architecture, locked in:

---

### The Three-Stage Pipeline (Final)

```
STAGE 1 — QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Satisfies course training requirement)
│
├── Dataset: LaMP News Headline Generation
│   (2,376 users, ~287 articles/user, already structured)
│
├── Training objective (Option B):
│   Prompt:   "Generate a headline in the style of user_{id}:
│              {article_content}"
│   Response: {real_headline}
│
├── What this achieves:
│   - Model learns to associate user identity with stylistic patterns
│   - Backpropagation happens here → course requirement satisfied
│   - Fine-tuned weights now encode style-awareness by design
│   - Activations from THIS model carry style signal, not generic signal
│
└── Output: fine-tuned LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct checkpoint


STAGE 2 — StyleVector Extraction (Paper replication + extension)
│
├── Generic model Mg: Gemini 2.0 Flash API
│   For every article → generate style-agnostic headline
│
├── Activation extraction on fine-tuned model M:
│   Positive: h_l(article ⊕ real_headline)      ← user-authentic
│   Negative: h_l(article ⊕ agnostic_headline)  ← style-stripped
│   Style vector = mean(positive - negative)
│
├── Two populations extracted:
│   Rich authors  (LaMP users ≥60 articles + Indian rich journalists)
│   Sparse authors (Indian journalists with 5-20 articles) ← cold-start targets
│
└── Output: one .npy vector per author


STAGE 3 — Cold-Start Interpolation (Novel contribution)
│
├── Input: rich-author style vectors (200+ LaMP + 30-40 Indian)
├── PCA → 50 dimensions (before clustering, not after)
├── KMeans sweep K=5 to 20, pick by silhouette score
│
├── For each sparse Indian author:
│   sparse_vec = noisy style vector from 5-20 articles
│   nearest_centroid = argmax cosine_similarity(sparse_vec, centroids)
│   final_vec = α * nearest_centroid + (1-α) * sparse_vec
│   → sweep α = 0.2 to 0.8 on validation set
│
└── Output: interpolated style vectors for sparse authors


EVALUATION TABLE (your core academic deliverable)
┌─────────────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┐
│ Method                  │ Rich Authors     │ Sparse Authors   │
├─────────────────────────┼──────────────────┼──────────────────┤
│ No personalization      │ baseline         │ baseline         │
│ RAG (BM25)             │ result           │ result           │
│ StyleVector (paper)     │ result           │ degrades ←key    │
│ Cold-Start StyleVector  │ result           │ improves ←key    │
└─────────────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────┘
Metrics: ROUGE-L, METEOR, BERTScore
```

---

### Revised 6-Week Plan

| Week | Focus | Key Deliverable | GPU Hours |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repo + Indian scraper + LaMP download | Clean dataset, author registry | 0 (CPU) |
| 2 | Data pipeline, splits, Gemini agnostic headlines | All splits saved, agnostic headlines generated | 0 (CPU) |
| 3 | QLoRA fine-tuning on LaMP | Fine-tuned checkpoint on HF Hub | ~10h L4 |
| 4 | Activation extraction (both datasets) | .npy vectors for all authors | ~12h L4 |
| 5 | Cold-start module + evaluation | Result table complete | ~5h L4 |
| 6 | FastAPI + frontend + deployment | Live URL, cached demo | ~3h L4 |

**Total: ~30 GPU hours on L4 = ~$14.40. Well inside your 75-100 credit budget.**

---

### Why This Architecture Is Stronger Than The Original Plan

The original plan had you training a generic headline generator then trying to extract style from it — internally contradictory. This architecture trains a **style-aware model**, extracts style vectors from a model that has learned to care about style, and then demonstrates cold-start on Indian journalists as a domain transfer contribution. Every stage now has a clear purpose and the stages reinforce each other instead of fighting.

The LaMP data also gives you the exact paper baseline numbers to compare against in your evaluation table. That comparison is what makes it a research contribution rather than just an implementation.

---

## ➡️ NEXT STEP

We start Week 1 code now. I need two things from you:

**1. Give me one actual TOI article URL with a named individual author** — something like `timesofindia.com/author/sehjal-gupta/...` or any article page. I need to inspect the real HTML structure before writing the scraper. Don't give me a homepage, give me an actual article.

**2. Same for HT** — one Hindustan Times article URL with a named author.

These two URLs determine the entire scraper architecture. Everything else — repo setup, config, utilities — I've already written in the previous session. You can copy those in while I wait for the URLs.

Good. I've now read every document — the StyleVector paper, the technical documentation (v1.0), and the full conversation history. I understand the complete project. Let me give you the definitive architecture.

First, the QLoRA clarification, because your answer to Question 3 revealed a misconception that will come up again.Now I'll lay out the complete, definitive architecture. Let me address the QLoRA confusion first, then give you the full plan visually.

**QLoRA IS weight fine-tuning.** When you train with QLoRA, you are adding LoRA adapter matrices (ΔW = BA) to the frozen base model's weight matrices. Gradients flow through B and A during backpropagation — that *is* updating the model's effective weights. When you merge the adapters afterward, the result is a literally different model from the base. Your course rubric requirement of "backpropagation + weight updates" is 100% satisfied by QLoRA. There is no conflict.

What you actually chose with "Option B + finetune weights" is: **QLoRA with author-conditioned prompts** — training the model to know that different authors have different styles. This is the correct and complete answer.

Here's the full architecture:Every box is clickable for more detail. Now let me walk through each key decision so you understand *why* each piece is what it is.

---

**The model: LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, not LLaMA-2-7B.**

The documentation (v1.0) uses LLaMA-2-7B because that's what the paper used. But the paper used LLaMA-2 because it was the best available at the time. You are not obligated to match the paper's model — you're *extending* it. LLaMA-3.1-8B is ungated (download right now, no Meta approval form), has a 128K context window (LLaMA-2 has 4K — Indian news articles will hit this limit), and produces cleaner internal representations. Your style vectors will be more separable in its activation space, which is the geometric property your cold-start clustering depends on. If your faculty asks why you didn't use LLaMA-2, the answer is: "We chose LLaMA-3.1-8B because it removes the access bottleneck, eliminates context-length truncation for long articles, and its improved representation geometry makes activation-space clustering more reliable — which is the core novel contribution."

**The training objective: author-conditioned, not generic.**

Your documentation (v1.0) has Stage 1 training on generic prompts: `"Generate a headline for this article: {article}"`. This is the flaw flagged in Session 1 — you're training the model to be style-blind, then trying to extract style from it. The fix is Option B: train on `"Write a headline in the style of {author_name}: {article}" → {real headline}`. This makes the model learn that different names correspond to different stylistic outputs. When you then extract activations from this model, they are from a model that has learned to care about authorial style — so the contrastive difference carries a cleaner style signal.

**The datasets: LaMP as primary, All The News for training, Indian for cold-start.**

LaMP's News Headline Generation task gives you 2,376 users with 287 articles each on average. That's your rich-author pool for style vector extraction and clustering. It also gives you direct comparability with Table 2 in the StyleVector paper. All The News V2 gives you the author-conditioned training data for QLoRA — it has named bylines, diverse topics, and large volume. Indian TOI/HT data is your sparse-author test set for the cross-cultural cold-start finding.

**The cold-start mechanism: PCA first, then KMeans.**

This is explicitly different from clustering raw 4096-dimensional vectors. KMeans in 4096D does not converge to meaningful clusters — distance metrics become unreliable at high dimensions (the curse of dimensionality). PCA to 50 dimensions first, *then* KMeans with silhouette score sweep. This is the technically defensible version.

---

**Revised 6-week plan:**

| Week | Focus | Key deliverable | GPU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repo setup, LaMP download, TOI/HT scraper | Clean datasets, author registry | 0h (CPU) |
| 2 | Data pipeline: schema unification, splits, Gemini agnostic headlines | All splits saved, agnostic CSVs complete | 0h (CPU) |
| 3 | QLoRA fine-tuning on All The News + LaMP | LLaMA-3.1-8B checkpoint on HF Hub | ~10h L4 |
| 4 | Activation extraction for all LaMP rich authors + Indian sparse authors | .npy vectors for all authors | ~12h L4 |
| 5 | Cold-start module (PCA → KMeans → interpolation) + full evaluation | Result table complete | ~5h L4 |
| 6 | FastAPI backend + React frontend + HF Spaces + Vercel deployment | Live URL, Colab live demo | ~3h L4 |

Total GPU: ~30h on Lightning AI L4. Well inside your credit budget.

---

**What to do right now, before writing any code:**

Give me these two things and I'll generate the Week 1 code immediately:

1. One actual URL from Times of India — an individual journalist's article page (not a homepage). Something like `timesofindia.com/blogs/author/sehjal-gupta/...` or any article with a named byline. I need to see the real HTML structure to write the scraper correctly.

2. Confirmation that you have a Lightning AI account (or create one now at `lightning.ai` — it takes 5 minutes and you need it before Week 3).

The repo structure, config files, data schema, and CI/CD skeleton from the documentation are already solid — those don't need changes. The only thing that changes from the v1.0 documentation is: LLaMA-2 → LLaMA-3.1-8B, and the training prompt format switches to author-conditioned. Everything else in your technical documentation is correct and stays.

**The one thing still blocked:** I still have no TOI or HT article URLs. That only blocks the scraper. Everything else — LaMP download, data pipeline, Gemini integration, QLoRA setup — I can start now without them.

**One thing nobody caught yet:** The LaMP-4 dataset uses user IDs (`user_1`, `user_2`...) not real journalist names. That means your author-conditioned prompt becomes `"Write a headline in the style of user_1247: {article}"`. This is fine — the model learns to associate IDs with styles, not real names. The Indian scraped data will have actual journalist names (`"Write a headline in the style of Sehjal Gupta: {article}"`), which creates a distribution mismatch at inference. You need a unified author-ID scheme. I've handled this in the pipeline below.

---

## 🧭 APPROACH — The Definitive Architecture

```
PROJECT: Cold-Start StyleVector
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STAGE 1: QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Week 3, ~10h L4)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Base:     LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct
Data:     LaMP-4 (2,376 users × 287 articles avg)
Prompt:   "Write a headline in the style of {author_id}: {article}" → {real_headline}
Method:   QLoRA 4-bit (bitsandbytes), LoRA rank=16, α=32
Purpose:  Model learns style-awareness → activations carry style signal
Output:   Fine-tuned checkpoint saved to HF Hub + Lightning Studio

STAGE 2: Style Vector Extraction (Week 4, ~12h L4)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Mg:       Gemini 2.0 Flash → style-agnostic headline per article
M:        Fine-tuned LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct
Extract:  a_pos = h_l(article ⊕ real_headline)
          a_neg = h_l(article ⊕ agnostic_headline)
          style_vec = mean(a_pos - a_neg) across all user articles
Target:   Layer ℓ ∈ {15..28} — sweep on val set (paper: middle-to-late layers)
Output:   One .npy vector per author → author_vectors/ directory

STAGE 3: Cold-Start Interpolation (Week 5, CPU)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Rich pool:   LaMP users with ≥50 articles (200+ users)
Sparse set:  Indian TOI/HT journalists with 5–20 articles
Step 1:  PCA(n=50) on rich-author vectors
Step 2:  KMeans(K) sweep silhouette → pick best K
Step 3:  For each sparse author:
           centroid = nearest cluster centroid (cosine sim)
           final = α × centroid + (1-α) × sparse_vec
           α sweeps 0.2 → 0.8 on val set

EVALUATION TABLE (your academic deliverable)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Method               | Rich Authors | Sparse Authors
─────────────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────
No personalization   | baseline     | baseline
RAG (BM25)          | result       | result
StyleVector (paper)  | result       | degrades ← key
Cold-Start StyleVec  | result       | improves ← novel
─────────────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────
Metrics: ROUGE-L, METEOR, BERTScore
```

---

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: file-reading
     3	description: "Use this skill when a file has been uploaded but its content is NOT in your context — only its path at /mnt/user-data/uploads/ is listed in an uploaded_files block. This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block — you already have it."
     4	compatibility: "claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork — any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/"
     5	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     6	---
     7	
     8	# Reading Uploaded Files
     9	
    10	## Why this skill exists
    11	
    12	When a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,
    13	the file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path
    14	in an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**
    15	You must go read it.
    16	
    17	The naive thing — `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` — is wrong for
    18	most files:
    19	
    20	- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.
    21	- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.
    22	- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.
    23	- On an image it does nothing useful at all.
    24	
    25	This skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to
    26	hand off to a deeper skill.
    27	
    28	## General protocol
    29	
    30	1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.
    31	2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.
    32	   ```bash
    33	   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    34	   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    35	   ```
    36	3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked
    37	   "how many rows are in this CSV", don't load the whole thing into
    38	   pandas — `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,
    39	   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain
    40	   embedded newlines).
    41	4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells
    42	   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced
    43	   operations that this skill does not.
    44	
    45	## Dispatch table
    46	
    47	| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |
    48	| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
    49	| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |
    50	| `.docx`                           | `pandoc` to markdown                                 | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    51	| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first — pandoc cannot read it     | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    52	| `.xlsx`, `.xlsm`                  | `openpyxl` sheet names + head                        | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    53	| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine="xlrd")` — openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    54	| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine="odf")` — openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    55	| `.pptx`                           | `python-pptx` slide count                            | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    56	| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first — python-pptx rejects it    | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    57	| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | — (below)                                 |
    58	| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | — (below)                                 |
    59	| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | — (below)                                 |
    60	| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | — (below)                                 |
    61	| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \| head` — no manifest to list                 | — (below)                                 |
    62	| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `pandoc` to plain text                               | — (below)                                 |
    63	| `.rtf`                            | `pandoc` (needs 3.1.7+) or soffice via docx skill    | — (below)                                 |
    64	| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | — (below)                                 |
    65	| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | —                                         |
    66	
    67	---
    68	
    69	## PDF
    70	
    71	**Never** `cat` a PDF — it prints binary garbage.
    72	
    73	Quick first move — get the page count and check if text is extractable:
    74	
    75	```bash
    76	pdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    77	pdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20
    78	```
    79	
    80	Then peek at the text content:
    81	
    82	```python
    83	from pypdf import PdfReader
    84	r = PdfReader("/mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf")
    85	print(f"{len(r.pages)} pages")
    86	print(r.pages[0].extract_text()[:2000])
    87	```
    88	
    89	For anything beyond a quick peek — figures, tables, attachments,
    90	forms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy
    91	— go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers
    92	content inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded
    93	content extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.
    94	
    95	For PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,
    96	go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
    97	
    98	---
    99	
   100	## DOCX / DOC
   101	
   102	The `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.
   103	Read it if you need any of those. For a quick look:
   104	
   105	```bash
   106	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx -t markdown | head -200
   107	```
   108	
   109	Legacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first — see the `docx`
   110	skill.
   111	
   112	---
   113	
   114	## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets
   115	
   116	The `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read
   117	it if you need any of those. For a quick look at `.xlsx` / `.xlsm`:
   118	
   119	```python
   120	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   121	wb = load_workbook("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx", read_only=True)
   122	print("Sheets:", wb.sheetnames)
   123	ws = wb.active
   124	for row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):
   125	    print(row)
   126	```
   127	
   128	`read_only=True` matters — without it, openpyxl loads the entire
   129	workbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust
   130	`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the
   131	dimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row
   132	count, iterate or use pandas.
   133	
   134	**Legacy `.xls`** — openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:
   135	
   136	```python
   137	import pandas as pd
   138	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls", engine="xlrd", nrows=5)
   139	```
   140	
   141	**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** — openpyxl also rejects this. Use:
   142	
   143	```python
   144	import pandas as pd
   145	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods", engine="odf", nrows=5)
   146	```
   147	
   148	---
   149	
   150	## PPTX
   151	
   152	```python
   153	from itertools import islice
   154	from pptx import Presentation
   155	p = Presentation("/mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx")
   156	print(f"{len(p.slides)} slides")
   157	for i, slide in enumerate(islice(p.slides, 3), 1):
   158	    texts = [s.text for s in slide.shapes if s.has_text_frame]
   159	    print(f"Slide {i}:", " | ".join(t for t in texts if t))
   160	```
   161	
   162	`p.slides` is not subscriptable — `p.slides[:3]` raises
   163	`AttributeError`. Use `islice` or `list(p.slides)[:3]`.
   164	
   165	**Legacy `.ppt`** — python-pptx only reads OOXML. Convert to `.pptx`
   166	first via LibreOffice; see `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the
   167	sandbox-safe `scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs
   168	here because the seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets
   169	LibreOffice uses for instance management).
   170	
   171	For anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.
   172	
   173	---
   174	
   175	## CSV / TSV
   176	
   177	**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell
   178	in row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:
   179	
   180	```python
   181	import pandas as pd
   182	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv", nrows=5)
   183	print(df)
   184	print()
   185	print(df.dtypes)
   186	```
   187	
   188	Approximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has
   189	RFC-4180 quoted newlines — the same quoted-cell case this section
   190	warned about above):
   191	
   192	```bash
   193	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv
   194	```
   195	
   196	Full analysis only after you know the shape:
   197	
   198	```python
   199	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv")
   200	print(df.describe())
   201	```
   202	
   203	TSV: same, with `sep="\t"`.
   204	
   205	---
   206	
   207	## JSON / JSONL
   208	
   209	Structure first, content second:
   210	
   211	```bash
   212	jq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   213	jq 'if type == "array" then length elif type == "object" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   214	```
   215	
   216	(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots — a bare `"hello"` or `42` is valid
   217	JSON per RFC 7159 — so guard the branch.)
   218	
   219	Then drill into what the user actually asked about.
   220	
   221	JSONL (one object per line) — do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line
   222	by line:
   223	
   224	```bash
   225	head -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .
   226	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl
   227	```
   228	
   229	---
   230	
   231	## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)
   232	
   233	**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your
   234	context as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You
   235	do not need to read them from disk to describe them.
   236	
   237	The disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image
   238	programmatically:
   239	
   240	```python
   241	from PIL import Image
   242	img = Image.open("/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg")
   243	print(img.size, img.mode, img.format)
   244	```
   245	
   246	For OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):
   247	
   248	```python
   249	import pytesseract
   250	print(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))
   251	```
   252	
   253	Note: the client resizes images larger than 2000×2000 down to that
   254	bound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not
   255	be the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;
   256	if the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.
   257	
   258	---
   259	
   260	## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)
   261	
   262	**List first. Extract never — unless the user explicitly asks.**
   263	Archives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.
   264	
   265	```bash
   266	unzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip
   267	tar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar
   268	```
   269	
   270	GNU tar auto-detects compression — `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,
   271	`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.
   272	
   273	If the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:
   274	
   275	```bash
   276	unzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt
   277	```
   278	
   279	**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file — there is
   280	no manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:
   281	
   282	```bash
   283	zcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50
   284	```
   285	
   286	---
   287	
   288	## EPUB / ODT
   289	
   290	```bash
   291	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub -t plain | head -200
   292	```
   293	
   294	For long ebooks, pipe through `head` — you rarely need the whole thing
   295	to answer a question.
   296	
   297	---
   298	
   299	## RTF
   300	
   301	Pandoc's RTF reader was added in 3.1.7 (Oct 2023). Debian Bookworm
   302	ships 2.17, so try pandoc first but expect it may fail:
   303	
   304	```bash
   305	pandoc /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf -t plain | head -200
   306	```
   307	
   308	If you see `Unknown input format rtf`, convert via LibreOffice using
   309	the sandbox-safe wrapper — see `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md` for
   310	`scripts/office/soffice.py` (do not call bare `soffice`; see the PPTX
   311	section above for why).
   312	
   313	---
   314	
   315	## Plain text / code / logs
   316	
   317	Check the size first:
   318	
   319	```bash
   320	wc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   321	```
   322	
   323	- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.
   324	- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user
   325	  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing
   326	  only if you genuinely need all of it.
   327	
   328	For log files, the user almost always cares about the end:
   329	
   330	```bash
   331	tail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   332	```
   333	
   334	---
   335	
   336	## Unknown extension
   337	
   338	```bash
   339	file /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin
   340	xxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5
   341	```
   342	
   343	`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`
   344	says "data" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the
   345	user what it is instead of guessing.

{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**Work context**

Dharmik is an M.Tech ICT (Machine Learning) student at DA-IICT, Gandhinagar, currently in his 2nd semester (MTech-2025 batch, Student ID: 202311039). He also serves as a Teaching Assistant for MC-124 (DSA Lab). He is actively preparing for the DA-IICT Placement Drive 2026–27, targeting ML / Computer Vision / AI roles.

**Personal context**

Dharmik is a 2003-born resident of Gandhinagar, Gujarat, with roots in the Anand area. His native language is Gujarati; he uses English for academic and professional work. He completed his B.E. in Computer Engineering from CVM University / MBIT (CGPA 8.53) before starting his M.Tech. He is a ChatGPT Plus subscriber and has opted out of ChatGPT data training.

**Top of mind**

Dharmik's primary focus is his Driver Drowsiness & Distraction Detection project (V2), which uses a dual-head binary CNN with MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0, ResNet-18, and a Custom CNN, and recently required preparing a comprehensive mid-evaluation report for faculty. He is also actively building and refining AI meta-prompts — including a Google Gemini Gem for prompt generation and a Master ML/DL Prompt — reflecting a broader interest in structured AI-assisted workflows.

**Brief history**

*Recent months*

- **Driver Drowsiness Detection (V1 → V2):** V1 failed due to inconsistent input formats; V2 rebuilt as a dual-head binary CNN using MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0, ResNet-18, and Custom CNN, trained on FL3D, Mendeley, and YawDD datasets with a masked loss function. Key engineering milestones include 8 bug fixes and GPU utilization improvements. Cross-domain evaluation on DMD dataset is in progress. Dharmik uses `@mention` folder syntax in his agent workflow and prefers Gemini 2.5 Pro (High) for long document generation with Claude as fallback.
- **Gemini Gem / Meta-Prompt Builder:** Built a comprehensive meta-prompt for a Gemini Gem that generates prompts across creative writing, coding, data analysis, and image/video generation for use across Gemini, ChatGPT, and Claude. Key design decisions: labeled multiple-choice clarifying questions, max two question rounds, dual output (Standard Markdown + Advanced XML Megaprompt), archetype detection before generation.
- **Master ML/DL Prompt (MASTER_ML_DL_PROMPT.txt v3.0):** Created a reusable prompt document for AI-assisted ML projects covering the full pipeline (EDA through deployment). Includes an "honest advisor" persona, Kaggle-specific OOF workflow, experiment tracking consolidated in Phase 8, and an intake robustness rule. Dharmik pushed for direct critical feedback over validation throughout.
- **Indian News Article Dataset:** Built a scraping pipeline for LLM fine-tuning targeting author-style personalized headline generation. Sources: TOI, Hindustan Times, NDTV, The Hindu, India Today, Cricbuzz. Two pipelines: RSS-driven (`news_scraper/`) and API-based (`api_approach/` using newsdata.io + trafilatura). Dynamic author discovery replaced hardcoded IDs after a correction. Comprehensive Word documentation produced.
- **Placement prep:** Using Striver A-Z DSA sheet; PPT-1 covered Math, Greedy, Binary Search, Hashing; PPT-2 covered Number Theory, Stack/Queue, Tree. Prefers C++ with arrays and strings for DSA/lab problems.
- **LLM Personalization (Minor Project):** Implementing LaMP benchmark (LaMP-4, LaMP-5) from scratch with RAG for personalized headline/title generation. Intended to serve as both minor and major M.Tech project.

*Earlier context*

- **PPE Kit Detection:** YOLOv8 nano trained on 11 classes for PPE compliance detection; 1000 epochs on RTX 2050 locally in VS Code; evaluated on 452 test images. Submitted as M.Tech project.
- **Kidney Disease Prediction (CKD):** KNN pipeline on UCI CKD dataset; IEEE LaTeX paper draft created. Identified 100% accuracy as overfitting due to small dataset size.
- **HackerRank Leaderboard Automator:** TA tool automating score extraction and Google Sheets updates for MC-124 DSA Lab.
- **Gradient Descent Convergence Analysis:** Manual implementation (no sklearn) of linear/polynomial regression with PCA and bias-variance analysis on cigar-shaped dataset.
- **Image Processing and GloVe Labs:** DFT, filtering, CLAHE, CNN/transfer learning; GloVe embeddings pipeline. Both completed as DA-IICT lab assignments.
- **Green Hydrogen / Blue Carbon Hackathon:** Real-time oceanic threat detection using copernicusmarine API, ML threat classification, and alert triggering. Built during a 12-hour hackathon.

*Long-term background*

- **B.E. projects (completed, deprioritized for resume):** Hand Gesture Video Controller (Python, OpenCV, MediaPipe — strongest resume entry), Simple Desktop Assistant, Wardrobe Tracker (MERN stack), Android apps (Todo, Tic Tac Toe).
- **Web dev internship:** Learned HTML/CSS/JS, built standard mini-projects, then React and MERN basics.
- **GATE scores:** CSE 2024: 519 / AIR 4735; CSE 2025: 610 / AIR 2928. Improvement reflects sustained preparation effort.

---

**Other instructions**

- **Global advisor rule (set 2026-03-09):** Act as an honest advisor — do not validate, challenge thinking, question assumptions, expose blind spots, and communicate weaknesses directly and without filtering.
- **Explain from scratch before advancing** (set 2025-08-14); break down algorithms and math step-by-step (2025-08-19); go section by section with detailed explanations when preparing (not revising) (2026-01-22).
- **Exam prep style:** Teach from absolute beginner to exam-ready advanced level — fundamentals first, then mathematical/technical, then exam-oriented (2026-02-08).
- **Assignment prompt style:** Break down goal and problem in simple terms, explain all required concepts from scratch assuming complete beginner (2026-02-04).
- **Code defaults:** Notebook-oriented (Jupyter/Colab, cell-by-cell); use sklearn unless told otherwise; keep code simple; C++ with arrays and strings for DSA/lab problems.
- **Implementation plans:** Provide detailed, structured plans with verification steps before any coding begins (2026-01-28).
- **VBA/PPT code:** Only enrich slides where content feels insufficient; keep planned fonts, no additional designs; do not include paths for screenshots or images (2026-03-29).
- **Academic reports:** Style like research papers (Islam 2020 as reference format); write in LaTeX (IEEEtran).
- **Coursera financial aid answers:** Tailor to the specific course name mentioned when asked (2025-08-21).
- **Hardware/environment:** RTX 2050 + i5-13240H laptop, Windows OS, Conda for environment management, VS Code for local training, Kaggle notebooks for GPU-heavy tasks (save/download outputs before session resets).

content.text:
Good. I've read all three conversations, the memories file, the StyleVector paper, and the technical doc. I know this project completely. Here's the consolidated state before I write a single line.

---

## ⚠️ REVIEW — State of This Project After Reading Everything

**What's confirmed and locked, no further debate:**

| Decision | Answer |
|---|---|
| Model M (steered) | LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct (ungated, 128K ctx) |
| Model Mg (generic) | Gemini 2.0 Flash API (free tier) |
| Training paradigm | QLoRA, author-conditioned prompts — Option B |
| Primary training data | LaMP-4 News Headline Generation (2,376 users, 287 articles/user avg) |
| Cold-start test set | Indian TOI + HT sparse journalists |
| Clustering approach | PCA → 50D FIRST, then KMeans (silhouette sweep K=5–20) |
| Platform | Lightning AI L4 for training + extraction; Kaggle for cheap debug runs |
| Budget | 75–100 credits ≈ ~$36–48. ~30 credits needed. 45–70 in buffer. |
| Status | Zero code written |

**The one thing still blocked:** I still have no TOI or HT article URLs. That only blocks the scraper. Everything else — LaMP download, data pipeline, Gemini integration, QLoRA setup — I can start now without them.

**One thing nobody caught yet:** The LaMP-4 dataset uses user IDs (`user_1`, `user_2`...) not real journalist names. That means your author-conditioned prompt becomes `"Write a headline in the style of user_1247: {article}"`. This is fine — the model learns to associate IDs with styles, not real names. The Indian scraped data will have actual journalist names (`"Write a headline in the style of Sehjal Gupta: {article}"`), which creates a distribution mismatch at inference. You need a unified author-ID scheme. I've handled this in the pipeline below.

---

## 🧭 APPROACH — The Definitive Architecture

```
PROJECT: Cold-Start StyleVector
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STAGE 1: QLoRA Fine-Tuning (Week 3, ~10h L4)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Base:     LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct
Data:     LaMP-4 (2,376 users × 287 articles avg)
Prompt:   "Write a headline in the style of {author_id}: {article}" → {real_headline}
Method:   QLoRA 4-bit (bitsandbytes), LoRA rank=16, α=32
Purpose:  Model learns style-awareness → activations carry style signal
Output:   Fine-tuned checkpoint saved to HF Hub + Lightning Studio

STAGE 2: Style Vector Extraction (Week 4, ~12h L4)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Mg:       Gemini 2.0 Flash → style-agnostic headline per article
M:        Fine-tuned LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct
Extract:  a_pos = h_l(article ⊕ real_headline)
          a_neg = h_l(article ⊕ agnostic_headline)
          style_vec = mean(a_pos - a_neg) across all user articles
Target:   Layer ℓ ∈ {15..28} — sweep on val set (paper: middle-to-late layers)
Output:   One .npy vector per author → author_vectors/ directory

STAGE 3: Cold-Start Interpolation (Week 5, CPU)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Rich pool:   LaMP users with ≥50 articles (200+ users)
Sparse set:  Indian TOI/HT journalists with 5–20 articles
Step 1:  PCA(n=50) on rich-author vectors
Step 2:  KMeans(K) sweep silhouette → pick best K
Step 3:  For each sparse author:
           centroid = nearest cluster centroid (cosine sim)
           final = α × centroid + (1-α) × sparse_vec
           α sweeps 0.2 → 0.8 on val set

EVALUATION TABLE (your academic deliverable)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Method               | Rich Authors | Sparse Authors
─────────────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────
No personalization   | baseline     | baseline
RAG (BM25)          | result       | result
StyleVector (paper)  | result       | degrades ← key
Cold-Start StyleVec  | result       | improves ← novel
─────────────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────
Metrics: ROUGE-L, METEOR, BERTScore
```

---

## 💻 CODE — Week 1: Complete Project Foundation

Everything below can be executed right now on your local machine (CPU only, free).

### Project Structure

content.name: create_file

input.description: Project config dataclass — all hyperparameters centralized
input.file_text:
"""
config.py — Single source of truth for all hyperparameters and paths.
No hardcoded values anywhere else in the project.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional


# ── Root paths ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROJECT_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent


@dataclass
class PathConfig:
    """All file paths. Change ROOT to move the entire project."""
    root: Path = PROJECT_ROOT
    data_raw: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw"
    data_interim: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "interim"
    data_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed"
    models: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "models"
    configs: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "configs"
    scripts: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "scripts"

    # Dataset files
    lamp_train: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_train.json"
    lamp_val: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_val.json"
    lamp_test: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_test.json"
    indian_raw: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "indian_articles.jsonl"

    # Processed
    lamp_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "lamp4_sft.jsonl"
    indian_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "indian_processed.jsonl"
    agnostic_headlines: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "interim" / "agnostic_headlines.jsonl"

    # Style vectors
    style_vectors_dir: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "models" / "style_vectors"

    def __post_init__(self):
        """Create all directories on first import."""
        for attr_name in vars(self):
            path = getattr(self, attr_name)
            if isinstance(path, Path) and not path.suffix:
                path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    """Model architecture and fine-tuning configuration."""
    # Base model — DO NOT use the base (non-instruct) variant
    model_name: str = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
    # Generic model for style-agnostic headline generation
    generic_model_name: str = "gemini-2.0-flash"  # Gemini Flash free tier

    # QLoRA configuration
    lora_r: int = 16          # LoRA rank — higher = more parameters, better fit
    lora_alpha: int = 32      # LoRA scaling factor (typically 2×rank)
    lora_dropout: float = 0.05
    # Which linear layers to apply LoRA to (standard for LLaMA)
    lora_target_modules: list = field(default_factory=lambda: [
        "q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",
        "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"
    ])
    bits: int = 4             # Quantization bits (4 = QLoRA, 8 = lighter quant)

    # Tokenizer
    max_length: int = 1024    # Max tokens per sample — LLaMA-3.1 supports 128K
    # but 1024 covers 99% of LaMP-4 articles


@dataclass
class TrainingConfig:
    """QLoRA fine-tuning hyperparameters."""
    # Batch sizes — tuned for L4 24GB with 4-bit quantization
    per_device_train_batch_size: int = 4
    per_device_eval_batch_size: int = 4
    gradient_accumulation_steps: int = 8   # Effective batch = 4×8 = 32
    # ↑ Do NOT reduce gradient_accumulation below 4 — training will be noisy

    # Learning rate — empirically good range for QLoRA on 7–8B models
    learning_rate: float = 2e-4
    lr_scheduler_type: str = "cosine"
    warmup_ratio: float = 0.05

    # Training duration — LaMP-4 is large, 3 epochs is sufficient
    num_train_epochs: int = 3
    max_steps: int = -1       # -1 means use num_train_epochs

    # Mixed precision — bf16 preferred on L4 (better than fp16 for LLMs)
    bf16: bool = True
    fp16: bool = False

    # Gradient checkpointing — saves ~30% VRAM at cost of ~20% compute
    gradient_checkpointing: bool = True

    # Saving
    save_strategy: str = "epoch"
    evaluation_strategy: str = "epoch"
    load_best_model_at_end: bool = True
    save_total_limit: int = 2
    metric_for_best_model: str = "eval_loss"

    # Output
    output_dir: str = str(PROJECT_ROOT / "models" / "qlora_checkpoint")
    logging_steps: int = 50
    report_to: str = "wandb"  # Switch to "none" to disable wandb

    # Reproducibility
    seed: int = 42
    data_seed: int = 42


@dataclass
class ExtractionConfig:
    """Style vector extraction configuration."""
    # Which layer(s) to extract from — paper finds middle-to-late layers optimal
    # For LLaMA-3.1-8B (32 layers), sweep layers 16–28
    # Default: layer 24 (75% depth) as starting point
    extraction_layer: int = 24
    layer_sweep: list = field(default_factory=lambda: list(range(16, 29)))

    # Steering strength at inference time
    alpha: float = 0.5
    alpha_sweep: list = field(default_factory=lambda: [0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5])

    # Style vector extraction function
    # Options: "mean_diff" | "logistic" | "pca"
    # Paper finding: mean_diff is simplest and works best (Fig 6)
    extraction_fn: str = "mean_diff"

    # Batch size for extraction (lower than training — we need activations in memory)
    batch_size: int = 1   # Process one (article, headline) pair at a time
    # ↑ Don't increase — each forward pass holds 4096-dim activations for every token

    # Minimum articles per author to compute a valid style vector
    # Authors below this threshold are "sparse" (cold-start targets)
    rich_author_min_articles: int = 50
    sparse_author_max_articles: int = 20


@dataclass
class ColdStartConfig:
    """Cold-start interpolation configuration."""
    # PCA — MUST happen before clustering, not after
    pca_components: int = 50   # Reduces 4096D → 50D before KMeans
    # ↑ Do NOT cluster in 4096D — distance metrics break at high dimension

    # KMeans sweep
    k_min: int = 5
    k_max: int = 20
    k_step: int = 1
    random_state: int = 42

    # Interpolation weight sweep
    alpha_min: float = 0.2
    alpha_max: float = 0.8
    alpha_step: float = 0.1
    # final_vec = alpha × centroid + (1-alpha) × sparse_vec

    # Similarity metric for nearest centroid
    similarity_metric: str = "cosine"   # "cosine" | "euclidean"


@dataclass
class GeminiConfig:
    """Gemini API configuration for style-agnostic generation."""
    model: str = "gemini-2.0-flash"
    # Prompt template — explicitly strips style context
    prompt_template: str = (
        "Generate a factual, neutral news headline for the following article. "
        "Do not use any specific journalistic style. Keep it concise.\n\n"
        "Article: {article}\n\nHeadline:"
    )
    max_output_tokens: int = 64
    temperature: float = 0.0  # Deterministic — we want style-free, not creative
    requests_per_minute: int = 60  # Gemini Flash free tier limit


@dataclass
class WandbConfig:
    """Weights & Biases experiment tracking."""
    project: str = "cold-start-stylevector"
    entity: Optional[str] = None   # Your wandb username
    tags: list = field(default_factory=lambda: ["qlora", "lamp4", "llama3.1"])


@dataclass
class Config:
    """Master config — import this everywhere."""
    paths: PathConfig = field(default_factory=PathConfig)
    model: ModelConfig = field(default_factory=ModelConfig)
    training: TrainingConfig = field(default_factory=TrainingConfig)
    extraction: ExtractionConfig = field(default_factory=ExtractionConfig)
    cold_start: ColdStartConfig = field(default_factory=ColdStartConfig)
    gemini: GeminiConfig = field(default_factory=GeminiConfig)
    wandb: WandbConfig = field(default_factory=WandbConfig)
    seed: int = 42


# Singleton — import CONFIG everywhere, never instantiate Config() yourself
CONFIG = Config()

config.py — Single source of truth for all hyperparameters and paths.
No hardcoded values anywhere else in the project.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional


# ── Root paths ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROJECT_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent


@dataclass
class PathConfig:
    """All file paths. Change ROOT to move the entire project."""
    root: Path = PROJECT_ROOT
    data_raw: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw"
    data_interim: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "interim"
    data_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed"
    models: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "models"
    configs: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "configs"
    scripts: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "scripts"

    # Dataset files
    lamp_train: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_train.json"
    lamp_val: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_val.json"
    lamp_test: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "lamp4_test.json"
    indian_raw: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw" / "indian_articles.jsonl"

    # Processed
    lamp_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "lamp4_sft.jsonl"
    indian_processed: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "indian_processed.jsonl"
    agnostic_headlines: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "interim" / "agnostic_headlines.jsonl"

    # Style vectors
    style_vectors_dir: Path = PROJECT_ROOT / "models" / "style_vectors"

    def __post_init__(self):
        """Create all directories on first import."""
        for attr_name in vars(self):
            path = getattr(self, attr_name)
            if isinstance(path, Path) and not path.suffix:
                path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    """Model architecture and fine-tuning configuration."""
    # Base model — DO NOT use the base (non-instruct) variant
    model_name: str = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
    # Generic model for style-agnostic headline generation
    generic_model_name: str = "gemini-2.0-flash"  # Gemini Flash free tier

    # QLoRA configuration
    lora_r: int = 16          # LoRA rank — higher = more parameters, better fit
    lora_alpha: int = 32      # LoRA scaling factor (typically 2×rank)
    lora_dropout: float = 0.05
    # Which linear layers to apply LoRA to (standard for LLaMA)
    lora_target_modules: list = field(default_factory=lambda: [
        "q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",
        "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"
    ])
    bits: int = 4             # Quantization bits (4 = QLoRA, 8 = lighter quant)

    # Tokenizer
    max_length: int = 1024    # Max tokens per sample — LLaMA-3.1 supports 128K
    # but 1024 covers 99% of LaMP-4 articles


@dataclass
class TrainingConfig:
    """QLoRA fine-tuning hyperparameters."""
    # Batch sizes — tuned for L4 24GB with 4-bit quantization
    per_device_train_batch_size: int = 4
    per_device_eval_batch_size: int = 4
    gradient_accumulation_steps: int = 8   # Effective batch = 4×8 = 32
    # ↑ Do NOT reduce gradient_accumulation below 4 — training will be noisy

    # Learning rate — empirically good range for QLoRA on 7–8B models
    learning_rate: float = 2e-4
    lr_scheduler_type: str = "cosine"
    warmup_ratio: float = 0.05

    # Training duration — LaMP-4 is large, 3 epochs is sufficient
    num_train_epochs: int = 3
    max_steps: int = -1       # -1 means use num_train_epochs

    # Mixed precision — bf16 preferred on L4 (better than fp16 for LLMs)
    bf16: bool = True
    fp16: bool = False

    # Gradient checkpointing — saves ~30% VRAM at cost of ~20% compute
    gradient_checkpointing: bool = True

    # Saving
    save_strategy: str = "epoch"
    evaluation_strategy: str = "epoch"
    load_best_model_at_end: bool = True
    save_total_limit: int = 2
    metric_for_best_model: str = "eval_loss"

    output_dir: str = str(PROJECT_ROOT / "models" / "qlora_checkpoint")
    logging_steps: int = 50
    report_to: str = "wandb"  # Switch to "none" to disable wandb

    # Reproducibility
    seed: int = 42
    data_seed: int = 42


@dataclass
class ExtractionConfig:

    # Which layer(s) to extract from — paper finds middle-to-late layers optimal
    # For LLaMA-3.1-8B (32 layers), sweep layers 16–28

    extraction_layer: int = 24
    layer_sweep: list = field(default_factory=lambda: list(range(16, 29)))

    # Steering strength at inference time
    alpha: float = 0.5
    alpha_sweep: list = field(default_factory=lambda: [0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5])

    # Options: "mean_diff" | "logistic" | "pca"

    # PCA — MUST happen before clustering, not after

chat_messages.text:
The user wants to discuss requirements, not code yet. Let me give a clean, direct summary of what's needed from them and what's needed for the project overall.Good call. Let me be direct about exactly where things stand.

---

## What I Know With Certainty (No More Debate)

The architecture is locked. Model, training approach, datasets, novel contribution, compute platform — all decided across your previous sessions. I'm not reopening any of that.

---

## What's Still Missing From Your Side

**Blocking (cannot proceed without these):**

One actual TOI article URL and one actual HT article URL — individual journalist pages, not homepages. This has been asked four times across two conversations. Without these, the scraper cannot be written. I cannot inspect HTML structure I've never seen.

**Non-blocking but needed before Week 3:**

LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct access approval from HuggingFace. You requested it — check if it's been granted. Go to `huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct` and see if the Download button is active or still says "Access Pending."

Gemini API key with Flash access. You need this for generating style-agnostic headlines. Free tier is sufficient. Get it from `aistudio.google.com`.

wandb account for experiment tracking. Free at `wandb.ai`. Without this, you cannot reproduce your best training run after 5 experiments.

Faculty confirmation on two things: whether the cold-start approach is approved as the novel contribution, and whether the full-stack deployment (FastAPI + React + HF Spaces) satisfies the "End-to-End ML application" rubric. You said this was "yet to discuss" — that conversation needs to happen in Week 1, not Week 5.

---

## What the Project Actually Requires Overall

Let me be direct about the real scope so you don't underestimate it.

**Data side:** You need LaMP-4 downloaded and processed into author-conditioned training pairs. You need a working scraper for TOI and HT that correctly extracts author name, article body, and headline. You need Gemini to generate one agnostic headline per article across your entire corpus — that's roughly 2,000–4,000 API calls minimum.

**Training side:** QLoRA fine-tuning on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct using LaMP-4. This is a real multi-hour GPU job. It needs to run on Lightning AI L4 without crashing, with checkpointing so a disconnect doesn't wipe 8 hours of work.

**Extraction side:** Running the fine-tuned model over every article-headline pair, saving the hidden states at a specific layer, computing contrastive differences, and storing one vector per author. This is the most compute-intensive part of the project (~12 GPU hours) and also the most error-prone — one indexing bug in the layer extraction code wipes all your vectors.

**Novel contribution side:** The cold-start module — PCA, KMeans, interpolation, alpha sweep. This is where your actual research claim lives. It has to produce a measurable improvement on sparse Indian authors. If the silhouette score is bad or the clusters aren't meaningful, your contribution fails. You need to know this in Week 5, not the night before submission.

**Evaluation side:** You need four methods evaluated — no personalization baseline, BM25 RAG, original StyleVector, and your cold-start version — across both rich LaMP authors and sparse Indian authors. That's a full 2×4 result table with ROUGE-L, METEOR, and BERTScore. This alone takes a full week if you don't plan it ahead.

**Deployment side:** FastAPI backend serving predictions from pre-computed style vectors, React frontend, deployed on HF Spaces and Vercel. The demo cannot run live LLM inference — it must serve from cached vectors and pre-generated outputs.

**Submission side:** GitHub repo, report in IEEE LaTeX format, presentation. All three need the results table finalized before you can write them.

---

## The Honest Timeline Risk

Six weeks is tight but achievable if nothing goes wrong. The two things most likely to blow your schedule are: the scraper taking longer than expected because TOI or HT has anti-scraping measures, and the activation extraction producing garbage vectors that require debugging the layer-indexing code. Both risks are highest in Weeks 1–4. Weeks 5–6 have no slack.

---

## What I Need From You Right Now

Just two things before any code gets written:

One TOI article URL. One HT article URL. Named individual journalist, article page, not author profile or homepage.

That's it. Everything else I can start the moment you give me those.

Good call. Let me be direct about exactly where things stand.

---

## What I Know With Certainty (No More Debate)

The architecture is locked. Model, training approach, datasets, novel contribution, compute platform — all decided across your previous sessions. I'm not reopening any of that.

---

## What's Still Missing From Your Side

**Blocking (cannot proceed without these):**

One actual TOI article URL and one actual HT article URL — individual journalist pages, not homepages. This has been asked four times across two conversations. Without these, the scraper cannot be written. I cannot inspect HTML structure I've never seen.

**Non-blocking but needed before Week 3:**

LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct access approval from HuggingFace. You requested it — check if it's been granted. Go to `huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct` and see if the Download button is active or still says "Access Pending."

Gemini API key with Flash access. You need this for generating style-agnostic headlines. Free tier is sufficient. Get it from `aistudio.google.com`.

wandb account for experiment tracking. Free at `wandb.ai`. Without this, you cannot reproduce your best training run after 5 experiments.

Faculty confirmation on two things: whether the cold-start approach is approved as the novel contribution, and whether the full-stack deployment (FastAPI + React + HF Spaces) satisfies the "End-to-End ML application" rubric. You said this was "yet to discuss" — that conversation needs to happen in Week 1, not Week 5.

---

## What the Project Actually Requires Overall

Let me be direct about the real scope so you don't underestimate it.

**Data side:** You need LaMP-4 downloaded and processed into author-conditioned training pairs. You need a working scraper for TOI and HT that correctly extracts author name, article body, and headline. You need Gemini to generate one agnostic headline per article across your entire corpus — that's roughly 2,000–4,000 API calls minimum.

**Training side:** QLoRA fine-tuning on LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct using LaMP-4. This is a real multi-hour GPU job. It needs to run on Lightning AI L4 without crashing, with checkpointing so a disconnect doesn't wipe 8 hours of work.

**Extraction side:** Running the fine-tuned model over every article-headline pair, saving the hidden states at a specific layer, computing contrastive differences, and storing one vector per author. This is the most compute-intensive part of the project (~12 GPU hours) and also the most error-prone — one indexing bug in the layer extraction code wipes all your vectors.

**Novel contribution side:** The cold-start module — PCA, KMeans, interpolation, alpha sweep. This is where your actual research claim lives. It has to produce a measurable improvement on sparse Indian authors. If the silhouette score is bad or the clusters aren't meaningful, your contribution fails. You need to know this in Week 5, not the night before submission.

**Evaluation side:** You need four methods evaluated — no personalization baseline, BM25 RAG, original StyleVector, and your cold-start version — across both rich LaMP authors and sparse Indian authors. That's a full 2×4 result table with ROUGE-L, METEOR, and BERTScore. This alone takes a full week if you don't plan it ahead.

**Deployment side:** FastAPI backend serving predictions from pre-computed style vectors, React frontend, deployed on HF Spaces and Vercel. The demo cannot run live LLM inference — it must serve from cached vectors and pre-generated outputs.

**Submission side:** GitHub repo, report in IEEE LaTeX format, presentation. All three need the results table finalized before you can write them.

---

## The Honest Timeline Risk

Six weeks is tight but achievable if nothing goes wrong. The two things most likely to blow your schedule are: the scraper taking longer than expected because TOI or HT has anti-scraping measures, and the activation extraction producing garbage vectors that require debugging the layer-indexing code. Both risks are highest in Weeks 1–4. Weeks 5–6 have no slack.

---

Just two things before any code gets written:

One TOI article URL. One HT article URL. Named individual journalist, article page, not author profile or homepage.

That's it. Everything else I can start the moment you give me those.

Short, direct answer about which dataset is best for each stage.**Stage 1 — QLoRA Fine-Tuning:**
**LaMP-4.** Already structured as (user_id, article, headline) triples. No preprocessing needed. 2,376 users × 287 articles = directly usable for author-conditioned training. All The News requires heavy preprocessing to extract bylines and match them to articles — extra work for no benefit here.

**Stage 2 — Style Vector Extraction (Rich Author Pool):**
**LaMP-4 again.** Same reason — user IDs are clean, history is pre-grouped per user, and using the same dataset as training means the model has already seen these authors' styles. Stronger style vectors result.

**Stage 3 — Cold-Start Test (Sparse Authors):**
**Your custom Indian dataset (TOI + HT).** This is the whole point of your novel contribution — showing the method works cross-domain on journalists the model has never seen. If you test cold-start on LaMP authors, you have no novelty claim. The Indian sparse journalists ARE the cold-start problem.

**Evaluation baseline comparison:**
**LaMP-4** for rich authors (direct comparison to the paper's Table 2), **custom Indian dataset** for sparse authors (your novel finding).

---

**Summary in one line each:**

| Stage | Dataset |
|---|---|
| QLoRA training | LaMP-4 |
| Style vector extraction | LaMP-4 |
| Cold-start testing | Custom Indian (TOI + HT) |
| Evaluation | Both |

All The News is not needed anywhere. Drop it.
